text: <par>The -y is a learned folk etymology; cf. Skt.</par>
<par>Note metathesis of Prefix and <latinform>C</latinform><latinform>d̀n4 i</latinform>. 2d syl = ‘speech’  < PTB *<latinform>s-ka-t</latinform>, STC #9 *<latinform>ka</latinform>.</par>
<par>This Aka form looks as if it has been metathesized from <latinform>mri</latinform>.</par>
<par>Contra DL:1195, Lahu <latinform>-phu-</latinform> in this compound seems to mean WHITE rather than the homophonous morpheme BULBOUS OBJECT.</par>
<par>M1: ;BP pfx? or 229? 1390? or 66? (presume <latinform>(k)a</latinform> because of <latinform>ʔə</latinform>, etc.)</par>
<par>according to DPTB p.29, <reconstruction>*aŋ > aŋ</reconstruction> in Dulong so unlikely that tag is correct.</par>
<par>STC erroneously lists the language for this form as "Nagari".</par>
<par>It is difficult to say whether Bodic forms like Tsangla <latinform>phak</latinform> are truly cognate to Tibetan <latinform>phag</latinform>, or rather loans from it.</par>
<par>cf. Jingpho last above? + below thru Nung</par>
<par>cf. Jingpho last above? + below thru Nung</par>

<par>STC has Lepcha <latinform>bryaŋ</latinform> 'name' "from <emph>sbraŋ</emph>" (p.31, fn.99). LaPolla has apparently interpreted this <latinform>sbraŋ</latinform> as an older Lepcha form, but it appears to be a PTB reconstruction (though missing an asterisk). Even Mainwaring's dictionary of Lepcha has 'name' as <latinform>a-bryaṅ</latinform> (1898:513).</par>
<par>If this Lahu form with voiced initial is cognate, it reflects a nasal prefix.</par>
<par>Lahu <latinform>kh-</latinform> is the regular reflex of <latinform>*kr</latinform>, and <latinform>-òʔ</latinform> is the regular reflex of the rhyme <latinform>*-ap</latinform>.</par>
